reference_answer: CMS #722113. Appears on its own line below "WARNING LETTER" in the body and again in the response-submission paragraph.
Khaleeq Ahmed, Owner. The body opens "Dear Mr. Ahmed:".
amd@nupacknutrition.com. Note that the email domain (nupacknutrition.com) differs from the website domain reviewed by FDA (www.unitechusk.com).
Issuing Office: Human Foods Program. Signed by Maria S. Knirk, JD, MBA, Director, Office of Enforcement, Office of Compliance and Enforcement, Human Foods Program.
United States Food and Drug Administration, Human Foods Program, Office of Enforcement, 5001 Campus Drive, College Park, Maryland 20740-3835.

Under the "Unapproved New Drugs" section, specifically inside the bulleted list following the line "Your firm's website, www.unitechusk.com, states the following under your MACA labeling:". The "[sic]" preserves a grammatical error in the original label.

Violation #4 cites 21 CFR Part 111, Subpart N. It covers written procedures for *returned* dietary supplements — including when returns must be destroyed, when they may be salvaged, requirements for reprocessing, release decisions, and investigation of other batches when a return implicates them.

It is first introduced in the opening paragraph of the body ("…review of your website, www.unitechusk.com…") and again in the "Unapproved New Drugs" section. It is *not* the same as the recipient's email domain (amd@nupacknutrition.com → nupacknutrition.com). The firm appears to operate under two distinct domains.

The "Unapproved New Drugs" section. Specifically, the Cleanse-CAMP claims appear under the prose lead-in "Your firm's Cleanse-CAMP label states the following:". Note that the formatting of the Cleanse-CAMP and Kidney Cleanse bullet blocks runs together in the source — the Kidney Cleanse lead-in sentence appears at the end of the second Cleanse-CAMP bullet line.
Maria S. Knirk, JD, MBA — Director, Office of Enforcement, Office of Compliance and Enforcement, Human Foods Program.

1. Cardiag 2. Diabetech 3. Cleanse-CAMP 4. MACA 5. Kidney Cleanse 6. Pure Pysyllium Husk (Note: the source spells the last product "Pure Pysyllium Husk" — likely a typo for "Pure Psyllium Husk," which is what the label section actually says. Both spellings appear in the source.)
Cardiag, Diabetech, and MACA.
Cardiag, Cleanse-camp, and MACA 1000 mg.

1. Failure to establish product specifications for identity, purity, strength, composition, and limits on contamination — 21 CFR 111.70(e) (with reference also to 21 CFR 111.73 and 21 CFR 111.75). 2. Failure to prepare and follow a written master manufacturing record for each unique formulation and batch size — 21 CFR 111.205(a). 3. Failure to prepare a batch production record every time a batch is manufactured — 21 CFR 111.255(a). 4. Failure to establish written procedures for returned dietary supplements — 21 CFR Part 111, Subpart N.

For Cleanse-CAMP: - "Cleanse-CAMP" — appears in the Unapproved New Drugs section opening list and the Cleanse-CAMP label sub-block. - "Cleanse-camp" — appears in the Adulterated Dietary Supplements section opening paragraph and in each of the four numbered CGMP violations. For Pure Psyllium Husk: - "Pure Pysyllium Husk" — appears in the Unapproved New Drugs opening list (twice, both with the same misspelling). - "Pure Psyllium Husk" — appears in the bulleted label-claim sub-block ("Your firm's label for your Pure Psyllium Husk states the following:"). The inconsistencies are present in the original FDA publication and are preserved verbatim.
August 20, 2025 through September 24, 2025.
January 12, 2026. The response requested an additional four weeks to provide a substantive reply.
The letter is dated March 20, 2026; the page-current date is April 28, 2026 — a difference of 39 calendar days.
15 working days from the date of receipt of the letter. The trigger is *receipt*, not the letter's signature date.

The letter names only two specific consequences: legal action including, "without limitation, seizure and injunction." Unlike some other FDA letters in this family, this letter does not enumerate Export Certificate withholding or new-application approval withholding. The "without limitation" phrasing means the named consequences are not exhaustive.

The letter reasons that these three products are intended for the treatment or prevention of diseases that are not amenable to self-diagnosis, treatment, or prevention without the supervision of a licensed practitioner. Because a layperson cannot safely self-treat the conditions implied by the labeling claims (cardiovascular disease, diabetes, erectile dysfunction, hypertension), no directions written for layperson use can be "adequate." This makes the products misbranded under section 502(f)(1) of the Act.

The response must include: - An explanation of *each step* being taken to *prevent recurrence* of violations (not just to address the current ones). - Copies of related documentation supporting the corrective actions. - If corrective actions cannot be completed within 15 working days, the reason for the delay and the timeline for completion. - If the firm believes its products are not in violation, its reasoning and supporting information.

| Product | Unapproved new drug | Misbranded drug | Adulterated dietary supplement | |---------|---------------------|------------------|--------------------------------| | Cardiag | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Diabetech | Yes | Yes | **Not named in adulterated section** | | MACA / MACA 1000 mg | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Cleanse-CAMP / Cleanse-camp | Yes | **Not named in misbranded section** | Yes | | Kidney Cleanse | Yes (listed in opening) | No | No | | Pure Pysyllium Husk / Pure Psyllium Husk | Yes (listed in opening) | No | No | So only Cardiag and MACA are classified into all three categories. Diabetech is unapproved-new-drug + misbranded but not named as adulterated. Cleanse-CAMP is unapproved-new-drug + adulterated but not named as misbranded. Kidney Cleanse and Pure Pysyllium Husk appear only in the opening unapproved-new-drugs list.

Sequence of events recoverable from the letter: - September 24, 2025 — Form FDA 483 issued at conclusion of inspection. - January 12, 2026 — firm responded asking for four more weeks. That is approximately 110 days (≈15.5 weeks) after the 483. - February 9, 2026 (approximately) — projected response date implied by the four-week request from January 12. - March 20, 2026 — warning letter signed. This is approximately 39 days after the projected extended response date. - As of the warning-letter date, FDA "has not received further correspondence" from the firm. So the warning letter is issued roughly six months after the FDA 483, after the firm's extension request expired without substantive follow-up. Note the answer depends on calendar arithmetic; the letter does not state these intervals explicitly.
